title: Fast-forward versus no-ff merges
description: A fast-forward just advances the branch pointer with no merge commit, while --no-ff always records a merge commit that preserves the branch's shape.
body: Use when deciding whether feature merges should leave a visible merge commit. Do not assume every merge creates a merge commit. When the target has no new commits, git fast-forwards by moving the pointer, leaving linear history; --no-ff forces a merge commit so the feature's boundary is recorded. Inspect git log --graph to see which happened. Validation signals include a topology that matches the team's policy, such as merge commits marking each merged PR. Risk boundary: a fast-forward erases the grouping of a feature, so use --no-ff when auditability of merges matters.
